coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-coupled with urchin-like tungsten-doped CoP as efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Meiling
Zhang, Qi
Geng, Mengyao
Cui, Yulong
Lu, Qing
Wang, Li
Du, Lingling
Yin, Guangchao
coredata.dc:description: Developing readily-available, highly-efficient and stable electrocatalysts for water splitting is a challenge. Herein, a novel non-precious metal catalyst via coupling the N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with the W doped CoP that is assembled on Ni foam (Wy-CoP@NCDsx/NF) has been developed as the efficient bifunctional catalysts for overall water splitting. Specifically, the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF catalyst exhibits typical urchin shape under the regulation of the NCDs, showing abundant active sites. Meanwhile, the W-doping modulates the electronic structure and lowers the energy barrier of the catalyst. Moreover, the porous Ni foam as the catalyst supporter enables to expose catalyst more active sites and accelerate electron transfer. Beneficially, the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF only requires 136 mV and 305 mV overpotentials in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to reach the current density of 100 mA·cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, respectively. More importantly, the electrolyzer assembled by such W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF consumes a cell potential as low as 1.664 V to afford a large current density of 100 mA·cm−2 with remarkable stability, which offers great promise for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CoP
W doping
Carbon dots
Overall water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical evaluation for overall water splitting

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration for the synthetic route of W-CoP@NCDs/NF catalyst; (b) XRD pattern, (c, d) SEM images, (e, f) TEM images, (g) HRTEM image and (h) EDS mapping of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF, respectively.
(a) XPS survey spectra and the high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) Co 2p and (c) P 2p of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 and CoP@NCDs0.5; (d) The high-resolution XPS spectra of W 4f of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5.

(a) HER polarization curves and (b) Tafel plots of CoP-NF, CoP@NCDs0.5/NF and CoP@NCDs1.0/NF samples in 1.0 M KOH, respectively; (c) HER polarization curves and (d) Tafel plots of CoP@NCDs0.5/NF, Pt/C/NF and W-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF with different W doping concentrations in 1.0 M KOH, respectively; (e) Double layer capacitances and (f) Nyquist plots of CoP@NCDs0.5/NF and W-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF with different W doping concentrations.

(a) OER polarization curves and (b) Tafel plots of CoP-NF, CoP@NCDs0.5/NF and CoP@NCDs1.0/NF samples in 1.0 M KOH, respectively; (c) OER polarization curves and (d) Tafel plots of CoP@NCDs0.5/NF, RuO2/NF and W-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF with different W doping concentrations in 1.0 M KOH, respectively.

(a) Polarization curves of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF || W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF for overall water splitting within a two-electrode cell in 1.0 M KOH; (b) Long-term durability tests of overall water splitting at the current density of 50 mA and 200 mA for W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF || W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF, respectively.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-coupled with urchin-like tungsten-doped CoP as efficient bifunctional electrocatalysts for overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Dongyue Future Hydrogen Energy Material Co., Ltd, Zibo 256401, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Dongyue Future Hydrogen Energy Material Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Dongyue Future Hydrogen Energy Material Co., Ltd, Zibo 256401, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing readily-available, highly-efficient and stable electrocatalysts for water splitting is a challenge. Herein, a novel non-precious metal catalyst via coupling the N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) with the W doped CoP that is assembled on Ni foam (Wy-CoP@NCDsx/NF) has been developed as the efficient bifunctional catalysts for overall water splitting. Specifically, the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF catalyst exhibits typical urchin shape under the regulation of the NCDs, showing abundant active sites. Meanwhile, the W-doping modulates the electronic structure and lowers the energy barrier of the catalyst. Moreover, the porous Ni foam as the catalyst supporter enables to expose catalyst more active sites and accelerate electron transfer. Beneficially, the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF only requires 136 mV and 305 mV overpotentials in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER) to reach the current density of 100 mA·cm−2 in 1.0 M KOH, respectively. More importantly, the electrolyzer assembled by such W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF consumes a cell potential as low as 1.664 V to afford a large current density of 100 mA·cm−2 with remarkable stability, which offers great promise for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The electrolytic water splitting is regarded as the greatly auspicious approach to achieve green and sustainable hydrogen (H2) production, which is composed of two half-reactions of hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Although the theoretical voltage for water splitting is 1.23 V [1], the additional power are still required to drive the reaction due to the slow water splitting kinetics [2]. Therefore, developing the lower-overpotential catalysts is vital to improve the energy-conversion efficiency. Currently, the noble metals are considered as the state-of-the-art electrocatalysts, while their scarcity, high price and poor durability severely limit their wide applications [3–5]. Thus, developing the low-cost, efficient and stable non-precious metal-based catalysts is crucial for the electrochemical water splitting [6–8].

Transition metal phosphides (TMPs) (e.g. CoP [9–16], Ni5P4 [17–21], MoP [22–25]) have attracted wide attentions in catalyzing water splitting, due to their suitable electron configuration, abundant chemical states, adjustable electronic structure and low cost [26,27]. However, these TMPs still suffers from the agglomeration, low electrical conductivity and poor corrosion resistance, deteriorating the catalytic behavior in overall water splitting [10,14,28–30]. To overcome these defects, many strategies have been explored on morphology optimization, element doping, energy-band regulation and so on [31–33]. For example, Xu et al. reported the ultra-fine CoPx nanoparticles tightly anchored onto well-arranged 2D carbon nanosheets that formed the porous 3D interconnected network structure, which shown a large surface area with numerous reaction sites, showing excellent three-function electrocatalytic activity towards OER, HER and ORR [34]. Li et al. constructed a Fe-doped mesopore CoP electrode (meso-FexCo1-xP/CC), and found Fe doping enables to enhance the adsorption of hydrogen at the Co site and reduce the dissociation energy of H2O, thus improving the intrinsic activity of the catalyst [35]. Huang et al. designed a co-phosphide oxide composite catalyst (CoP-CoxOy/CC), in which the electrons redistribution at the cobalt phosphide/oxide interface produced a synergistic effect, resulting in extremely high hydrogen evolution activity [36].

It is acknowledged that the carbon-based materials are widely employed in modulating catalysts due to their excellent chemical stability and electrical conductivity [9,37–41]. As a new type zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial, the carbon dots (CDs) are endowed with abundant functional groups on the surface, which can easily coordinate with metal ions to form 3D network structure and inhibit metal agglomeration [42]. Moreover, the N doping can further regulate the electronic structure of CDs and promote the electronic transfer between CDs and metal [43–45]. Therefore, N-doped CDs (NCDs) enable to be as the structural and conductive modulators of TMPs catalysts, which not only can improve the electron transfer, but also can be expected to regulate the morphology and increase surface active centers [46]. Furthermore, the proper cationic doping is promising to further modulate the electronic structure of catalysts and improve the catalytic activity. As is reported, the W atoms exhibit a small electronegativity (1.5 vs. 1.9 of Co) with a stable hexavalent state [47], and W doping into CoP can decrease the d-band center of the surface Co atoms, which is beneficial for the catalytic behavior [48]. Therefore, the collaborative optimization of NCDs and W doping would make up for the deficiency of TMPs and significantly enhance the catalytic activity of TMPs catalysts.

In this study, we reported a non-precious metal catalyst of N-doped carbon dots coupled with the W-doped CoP on the Ni foam (W-CoP@NCDs/NF) as the bifunctional catalysts towards HER and OER. Benefited by the regulating effect of NCDs on the morphology of CoP, the unique urchin-like CoP was formed that can expose abundant active sites. Meanwhile, the high conductivity of NCDs and the high-quality interface contact of CoP@NCDs effectively enhanced the electron transfer and reduced the reaction barrier. On this basis, the W doping further modulated the electronic structure to improve the adsorption and the dissociation of intermediates. As a result, the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF catalyst exhibits significantly promoted electrocatalytic activity for water splitting, with the overpotential of 305 mV and 136 mV for OER and HER to afford the 100 mA·cm−2 current density, respectively, resulting in only 1.664 V to drive overall water splitting with an outstanding stability.

The synthetic route of the W-CoP@NCDs/NF catalysts is illustrated in Fig. 1 a, and the experimental details are shown in the Supporting information. Firstly, the cobalt compounds and NCDs were assembled on the Ni foam (NF) by an hydrothermal approach. Subsequently, the as-prepared hybrid was immersed in Na2WO4 solution to introduce the W dopant. Finally, the W-CoP@NCDs/NF catalyst was obtained by phosphating treatment at 300 °C. In this process, the bare NF obviously changed from silver to lilac via hydrothermal treatment, and further changed into black by phosphating, indicating that the cobalt phosphide has been formed on the NF, which is confirmed by the X-ray power diffraction (XRD) characterization (Fig. 1b). Besides the signals from NF support, the diffraction peaks of W-CoP at 31.6°, 48.1°, 56.0° and 56.7° can be well indexed to the (011), (211), (020) and (301) plane of CoP (JCPDS:29–0497), respectively (Fig. 1b). No W-related diffractive peak is recognized, indicating the W dopant has no new crystalline phase during the phosphating process.

As reported, abundant hydroxyl and carbonyl groups as the anionic coordinating agent are formed on the surface of the NCDs, which would play an important role in regulating the morphology of CoP [49,50]. We can see that the CoP displays a typical divergent and robust nano-needle-like structure in the absence of NCDs (Fig. S1). While the concentration of NCDs is increased to 0.5 mg·mL−1, the obtained CoP on CoP@NCDs0.5/NF exhibits a typical urchin-like structure (Fig. S2). Such morphological modulation can be attributed the selective adsorption of hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of NCDs. When the NCDs are introduced into the hydrothermal reaction, the cobalt compounds and NCDs in the solution collide with the nanoneedle crystals on the surface through thermal motion and form new crystal nuclei. As a result, the NCDs would affect the recrystallization of CoP and reduce their surface energy, causing the CoP to accumulate in specific directions and form the urchin-like structure. While the excessive NCDs would break the balance, and the reduced surface energy would force the CoP to form the spherical structures [51]. As shown in Fig. S3, the microsphere-shaped CoP is formed on the CoP@NCDs1.0/NFs with the concentration of NCDs increasing to 1 mg·mL−1.

Based on the optimal urchin-shaped CoP@NCDs0.5, the W doping was further conducted. The SEM images (Fig. 1c and d) show the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 with 0.03 M of W dopant maintains urchin-like structure, which are densely distributed on the surface of NF with an average size of ∼ 3 μm (Fig. S4), which indicates that the W doping makes no difference to the morphology of catalysts. From the TEM images in Fig. 1e and f, one can clearly see that the urchin-like W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 presents many side branches protruding from the center, which could provide good channels for electron transfer between the active sites and electrolyte solution. Meanwhile, the unique urchin-like structure would expose larger surface area and more active sites, and provides enough space for gas escaping. In the HRTEM image (Fig. 1g), the well-defined lattice fringes with lattice spacings of 0.18 nm and 0.22 nm can be closely corresponded to the (211) plane of CoP and (100) plane of NCDs [43], respectively. Furthermore, the good interface contact between W-CoP and NCDs can be clearly observed, which can effectively regulate electron transfer and optimize the catalytic kinetics of the interface. Also, the EDS mapping analysis (Fig. 1h) implies that the Co, P, W, N and C elements are evenly dispersed on the NF, in which the N element originates from the NCDs (Fig.S5).

To further investigate the detailed elemental composition and chemical state, the catalyst was scraped from NF and characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the survey spectra further demonstrate the presence of W, C, Co, P, O and N in W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5, corresponding well with the EDS results. The high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p for CoP@NCDs0.5 and W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 are displayed in Fig. 2b. Specifically, the binding energies (BEs) at 777.6 eV and 792.9 eV are attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbitals of metallic Co from Co-P, the ones at 781.9 eV and 798.0 eV are attributed to the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbitals of Co2+from Co-O, and the others at 786.6 eV and 803.07 eV are the two satellite peaks. The high-resolution XPS spectra of P 2p for CoP@NCDs0.5 and W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 is presented in Fig. 2c, in which the broad peaks at 133.9 eV is derived from the surface oxidation of phosphide (P-O), and the BEs at 129 eV and 129.9 eV are attributed to the P 2p1/2 and P 2p3/2 of Co-P, respectively. Compared with the CoP@NCDs0.5, the BEs of Co 2p in the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 shows a negative shift of ∼ 0.2 eV, and the P 2p peaks also show a negative movement with W doping, which indicates that W atoms effectively regulate the local electron configuration of the catalyst. Meanwhile, the XPS spectrum of W 4f shows two identical peaks of W 4f7/2 at 35.4 eV and W 4f5/2 at 37.5 eV (Fig. 2d), indicating the main presence of W is in + 6 state (W6+) on the surface [47,52,53]. These findings reveal the pivotal role of W doping in enhanced HER catalytic property. On the one hand, the W atoms with the larger atomic radius replace the Co atoms with the smaller atomic radius, resulting in the original lattice distortion that increases the defect sites of the catalyst for intermediate adsorption. On the other hand, W doping further regulates the electronic structure and accelerates the electron transfer that is conducive to improving the HER performance [48,54,55]. In addition, in order to explore the influence of N-CDs on the catalyst, we compared the XPS of CoP/NF before and after N-CDs regulation, as shown in Fig. S6 and Fig. 2. The Co 2p3/2 in CoP/NF without N-CDs regulation and the Co-P bond in P 2p both increase by 0.3 eV compared with the CDs-regulated results. This indicates that the electron density in CoP increases after binding with N-CDs. Therefore, P atoms with more negative charge and strong electrostatic affinity act as proton acceptors, promoting hydrogen generation in HER process [28].

The HER catalytic properties of CoP/NF, CoP@NCDs0.5/NF, CoP@NCDs1.0/NF are firstly evaluated using a standard three-electrode system in 1.0 M KOH. The HER polarization curves in the Fig. 3 a show the overpotential of CoP@NCDs0.5/NF is 103 mV at the current density of 10 mA·cm−2, which is lower than these of CoP/NF (137 mV) and CoP@NCDs1.0NF (129 mV). Also, the CoP@NCDs0.5/NF shows excellent ability at large current density for HER (η100 = 188 mV, η200 = 224 mV, η300 = 245 mV), which is significant for practical applications. Meanwhile, the catalyst presents the smaller Tafel slope (86.3 mV·dec-1) among them (Fig. 3b), implying a faster HER rate. This can be attributed to the moderate incorporation of NCDs inducing the urchin-like structure formed in catalyst and the interfacial effects between CoP and NCDs, accelerating the electron transfer and optimizing the reaction kinetics. On this basis, it is expected to further enhance HER activity by W doping in the CoP@NCDs0.5/NF.

Compared with CoP@NCDs0.5/NF, we found that the HER overpotential of these catalysts with various amounts of W doping was significantly reduced (Fig. 3c), in which the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF displays the best HER activity with the lowest overpotential (56 mV) at 10 mA·cm−2 and (136 mV) at 100 mA·cm−2. Notably, although the commercial catalyst Pt/C/NF exhibits the lower overpotential at the low current density (e.g., 10 mA·cm−2), the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 exhibits a greater advantage in overpotential at the higher current density (e.g., 300 mA·cm−2). Also, the Tafel slope of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF is 79.1 mV·dec-1 (Fig. 3d), which is almost comparable to that of commercial Pt/C-NF (72.7 mV·dec-1), and lower than ones of other W-CoP@NCDs/NF and CoP@NCDs1.0/NF (86.3 mV·dec-1), showing a rapid HER rate. Further, the overpotentials and the Tafel slopes of various catalysts indicate that the catalyst with W doping of 0.03 M possesses the optimal HER performance. These results indicates that an appropriate doping of W atoms can effectively enhance the catalytic performance of CoP@NCDs0.5/NF. On the one hand, W doping can reduce the D-band center of Co, which is conducive to the desorption of H2O to decompose and produce the intermediate Hads. On the other hand, the intermediate Hads is more inclined to bind with W, and thus the efficient H2 production is obtained with W doping [48]. While introducing excess W dopants would induce strong surface adsorption and affect the H desorption, resulting in the reduced electrocatalytic activity of the catalyst [48]. Therefore, the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF was chosen to study its catalytic active of HER, OER and overall water splitting in detail.

To further investigate the charge transfer properties of the W-doped catalysts, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3f, W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF processes the smallest charge transfer resistance than others, suggesting a kinetically higher efficient charge transfer. Additionally, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was estimated by measuring the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) of these electrocatalysts. It can be found that the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF displays larger Cdl (16.8 mF·cm−2) than other catalysts (Fig. 3e and Fig. S7), indicating the larger ECSA and the higher intrinsic activity. This suggests that W doping effectively improves the intrinsic activity of the catalyst. Moreover, the long-term stability is crucial for the practical application of catalysts. The chronoamperometry and CV method were adopted to study the stability of the catalysts. After the 1000 cycle CVs, the LSV curves of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF only show slight degradation (Fig. S8a). Further, the chronoamperometry curve shows the current density retention rate with 83.7% within 11 h (Fig. S8b), which indicates that the catalyst is endowed with excellent stability for HER in alkaline medium.

Aside from the HER performance, the OER is another electrochemical half-reaction of water splitting, and the OER catalytic performance of these catalysts was also evaluated in 1.0 M KOH (Fig. 4 ). The OER catalytic performance of these catalysts show a similar trend to HER, in which the CoP@NCDs0.5/NF shows the superior OER catalytic activity (Fig. 4a), especially at the higher current density (>100 mA·cm−2), which possesses a lower overpotential of 348 mV at 100 mA·cm−2, less than ones of CoP@NCDs1.0/NF (356 mV@100 mA·cm−2) and CoP/NF (360 mV@100 mA·cm−2). Meanwhile, the CoP@NCDs0.5/NF presents a smaller Tafel slope (118.6 mV·dec-1) compared to the latter two (Fig. 4b), revealing the superior reaction kinetics.

Further, the OER polarization carves of various catalysts with W doping were also recorded in Fig. 4c. Compared with the commercial RuO2/NF (310 mV@10 mA·cm−2), the overpotential of CoP@NCDs/NF is lowered to 268 mV@10 mA·cm−2, while the one of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF is further lowered to 255 mV at 10 mA·cm−2, exhibiting the lower energy potential barrier for the oxygen evolution. Such superior OER performance of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF is also evident from the potential at a fixed largest current density. For instance, W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF just requires an overpotential of 305 mV to achieve the large current density of 100 mA·cm−2, significantly superior to other catalysts. Furthermore, the enhanced OER performance of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF is further confirmed by its Tafel slope of 60.2 mV·dec-1 (Fig. 4d), which is close to that of the commercial RuO2/NF (58.4 mV·dec-1), implying the more favorable reaction kinetics. After 1000 CV cycles, a slight overpotential lost (24 mV) occurs to W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF at 100 mA·cm−2 current density (Fig. S9a), and the chronoamperometry curve shows the current density of 50 mA·cm−2 retains 88.7% within 11 h (Fig. S9b), presenting the excellent stability for OER in alkaline medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical evaluation for overall water splitting
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the potential in practical applications, an alkaline electrolyzer using the optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF was assembled as both the cathode and anode (Fig. 5 a inset). Remarkably, the electrolyzer requires a cell voltage lowed to1.664 V to drive a current density of 100 mA·cm−2 (Fig. 5a). Also, the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF//W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF electrolyzer has been demonstrated with an excellent stability. As shown in Fig. 5b, the chronoamperometry curves demonstrate that W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF based electrolyzer can stably work both at a current density of 50 mA·cm−2 and the higher of 200 mA·cm−2 over 11 h without distinct catalyst agglomeration and current declines, demonstrating excellent stability. Also, the gas bubbles can be observed from the inset of Fig. 5a despite the internal resistance, indicating the W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF catalyst is endowed with great promise in overall water splitting. Further, Fig. S10 summaries the overpotential at 10 mA·cm−2 for W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF and other recently reported HER and OER electrocatalysts in 1 M KOH (details in Table S1 and S2). We can see that our catalyst has the competitive advantage in lower overpotential, indicating the stronger catalytic capacity towards HER [28].

In summary, we have developed a non-precious metal catalyst of W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5 supported on NF as the efficient bifunctional catalysts for overall water splitting. Benefiting from the modification of NCDs, the CoP crystals presents a unique urchin-shape that exposes large active surface. Meanwhile, the good conductivity of NF support and the interfacial effects between CoP and NCDs can further facilitate the electron transfer, thus improving the catalytic activity. Moreover, the water decomposition kinetics of the catalyst has been further improved by W doping that regulates the electronic structure and the adsorption–desorption behavior. Impressively, the obtained optimal W0.03-CoP@NCDs0.5/NF catalyst possesses excellent catalytic activity and stability for both HER and OER, which requires the overpotential of 136 mV for HER and 305 mV for OER to reach 100 mA·cm−2, showing great competition among these reported CoP-based catalysts. More importantly, it can be directly utilized as bifunctional catalysts at both anode and cathode for efficient overall water splitting, which achieve a current density of 100 mA·cm−2 with a low cell voltage of 1.664 V. Overall, this study might provide novel idea to the rational design of the efficient non-precious metal catalysts that will boost the development of the electrolytical water splitting for sustainable H2 production.

Meiling Sun: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Resources. Qi Zhang: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mengyao Geng: Data curation. Yulong Cui: Data curation. Qing Lu: Formal analysis. Li Wang: Conceptualization. Lingling Du: Writing – review & editing. Guangchao Yin: Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation Joint Innovation Fund of Shandong Province (ZR2021LFG019), National Natural Science Foundation of China (61904098, 11904209) and Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2019QF018).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcrysgro.2023.127430.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategies for developing transition metal phosphides as heterogeneous electrocatalysts for water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, B. Kong, D. Zhao, H. Wang, C. Selomulya, Strategies for developing transition metal phosphides as heterogeneous electrocatalysts for water splitting, Nano Today. 15 (2017) 26-55, 10.1016/j.nantod.2017.06.006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-supported transition-metal-based electrocatalysts for hydrogen and oxygen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Sun, Z. Yan, F. Liu, W. Xu, F. Cheng, J. Chen, Self-Supported Transition-Metal-Based Electrocatalysts for Hydrogen and Oxygen Evolution, Adv. Mater. 32 (2020) 1806326, 10.1002/adma.201806326.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tuning of Trifunctional NiCu bimetallic nanoparticles confined in a porous carbon network with surface composition and local structural distortions for the electrocatalytic oxygen reduction, oxygen and hydrogen evolution reactions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A. Ahsan, A.R. Puente Santiago, Y. Hong, N. Zhang, M. Cano, E. Rodriguez-Castellon, L. Echegoyen, S.T. Sreenivasan, J.C. Noveron, Tuning of Trifunctional NiCu Bimetallic Nanoparticles Confined in a Porous Carbon Network with Surface Composition and Local Structural Distortions for the Electrocatalytic Oxygen Reduction, Oxygen and Hydrogen Evolution Reactions, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 142 (2020) 14688-14701, 10.1021/jacs.0c06960.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical neutralization energy: from concept to devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ding, P. Cai, Z. Wen, Electrochemical neutralization energy: from concept to devices, Chem. Soc. Rev. 50 (2021) 1495-1511, 10.1039/D0CS01239D.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress made in the mechanism comprehension and design of electrocatalysts for alkaline water splitting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Hu, L. Zhang, J. Gong, Recent progress made in the mechanism comprehension and design of electrocatalysts for alkaline water splitting, Energy Environ. Sci. 12 (2019) 2620-2645, 10.1039/C9EE01202H.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Co–Fe–Cr (oxy)Hydroxides as Efficient Oxygen Evolution Reaction Catalysts
